# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/.agents/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- speech: Use when the user asks for text-to-speech narration or voiceover, accessibility reads, audio prompts, or batch speech generation via the OpenAI Audio API; run the bundled CLI (`scripts/text_to_speech.py`) with built-in voices and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. Custom voice creation is out of scope. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/speech/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/.agents/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- speech: Use when the user asks for text-to-speech narration or voiceover, accessibility reads, audio prompts, or batch speech generation via the OpenAI Audio API; run the bundled CLI (`scripts/text_to_speech.py`) with built-in voices and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. Custom voice creation is out of scope. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/speech/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/.agents/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- speech: Use when the user asks for text-to-speech narration or voiceover, accessibility reads, audio prompts, or batch speech generation via the OpenAI Audio API; run the bundled CLI (`scripts/text_to_speech.py`) with built-in voices and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. Custom voice creation is out of scope. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/speech/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/.agents/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- speech: Use when the user asks for text-to-speech narration or voiceover, accessibility reads, audio prompts, or batch speech generation via the OpenAI Audio API; run the bundled CLI (`scripts/text_to_speech.py`) with built-in voices and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. Custom voice creation is out of scope. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/speech/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. # AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. - pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/.agents/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- speech: Use when the user asks for text-to-speech narration or voiceover, accessibility reads, audio prompts, or batch speech generation via the OpenAI Audio API; run the bundled CLI (`scripts/text_to_speech.py`) with built-in voices and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. Custom voice creation is out of scope. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/speech/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

If a decision is necessary and cannot be discovered from local context, ask the user directly. However, in Default mode you should strongly prefer executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions.

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/.agents/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- speech: Use when the user asks for text-to-speech narration or voiceover, accessibility reads, audio prompts, or batch speech generation via the OpenAI Audio API; run the bundled CLI (`scripts/text_to_speech.py`) with built-in voices and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. Custom voice creation is out of scope. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/speech/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/.agents/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- speech: Use when the user asks for text-to-speech narration or voiceover, accessibility reads, audio prompts, or batch speech generation via the OpenAI Audio API; run the bundled CLI (`scripts/text_to_speech.py`) with built-in voices and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. Custom voice creation is out of scope. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/speech/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

# AGENTS

## Scope
This file is the root guidance for AI-driven work in the LeapScholar Android mono-repo. It describes repo-wide architecture, build expectations, and how to decide where to add deeper `CONTEXT.md` files.

## Repo Structure (Gradle Modules)
- `app`: main LeapScholar Android application (app shell + most user-facing features).
- `login-app`: login and authentication UI/flows as a library module.
- `app-common`: shared design system, network helpers, payment/login utilities, analytics.
- `android-ui-kit`: reusable UI components and design primitives.
- `pariksha-android`: test-prep / mock-test module.
- `bol-bachchan-android`: speaking/IELTS assessment module.
- `drishti-android`: marketing/analytics integration module.
- `benchmark`: macrobenchmark tests targeting `:app`.

## Architecture Expectations
This repo follows the layered flow from README:

```
View (Compose) -> ViewModel -> Use Case(s) -> Repository -> Data Source(s)
```

Guidelines to keep behavior consistent:
- UI is declarative Compose in new code. Keep Composables focused on rendering and user intents.
- ViewModels coordinate use cases and expose UI-ready state (`StateFlow`, `SharedFlow`, etc.).
- Use cases are the home for business rules and mapping.
- Repositories delegate to data sources and keep decision logic thin.

## Build & Run (Repo-Wide)
Use the Gradle wrapper and Java 17.

```
./gradlew :app:assembleDebug
./gradlew :app:installDebug
./gradlew test
./gradlew lint
```

Common variants:
- `debug`, `release`, `debugRelease` (app and some libraries)
- `benchmark` (macrobenchmark module)

## Local Setup
- Ensure `local.properties` has `sdk.dir` for the Android SDK.
- Java 17 is required for compilation.

## Where to Add `CONTEXT.md`
- Add a module-level `CONTEXT.md` at each Gradle module root.
- Add feature-level `CONTEXT.md` inside modules for folders that contain behavior, UI, or logic.
- Treat each `CONTEXT.md` as the context file for that package/feature so contributors can quickly understand what it is doing well, what is missing, and where to improve.
- Use `CONTEXT.md` as the source of truth whenever you need to understand what a package/feature does.
- Skip folders that only contain data classes or plain DTOs (for those, mention them in the parent `CONTEXT.md` and why they were skipped).

## Working Agreements for AI Changes
- Prefer small, safe increments and keep changes scoped to the module/feature you are editing.
- Update or add tests when behavior changes, and document how to run them.
- Do not rotate or replace API keys or endpoints without explicit product/security approval.
- If you add a new feature folder, also add a local `CONTEXT.md` describing its purpose and build/test path.


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- atlas: macOS-only AppleScript control for the ChatGPT Atlas desktop app. Use only when the user explicitly asks to control Atlas tabs/bookmarks/history on macOS and the "ChatGPT Atlas" app is installed; do not trigger for general browser tasks or non-macOS environments. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/atlas/SKILL.md)
- figma: Use the Figma MCP server to fetch design context, screenshots, variables, and assets from Figma, and to translate Figma nodes into production code. Trigger when a task involves Figma URLs, node IDs, design-to-code implementation, or Figma MCP setup and troubleshooting. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/figma/SKILL.md)
- implement-plan: Execute a user-approved implementation plan end-to-end by translating plan steps into concrete code changes, tests, and status updates. Use when the user asks to implement a plan, execute planned tasks, carry out roadmap items, or convert a checklist/spec into completed work. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/implement-plan/SKILL.md)
- openai-docs: Use when the user asks how to build with OpenAI products or APIs and needs up-to-date official documentation with citations (for example: Codex, Responses API, Chat Completions, Apps SDK, Agents SDK, Realtime, model capabilities or limits); prioritize OpenAI docs MCP tools and restrict any fallback browsing to official OpenAI domains. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/openai-docs/SKILL.md)
- pdf: Use when tasks involve reading, creating, or reviewing PDF files where rendering and layout matter; prefer visual checks by rendering pages (Poppler) and use Python tools such as `reportlab`, `pdfplumber`, and `pypdf` for generation and extraction. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/pdf/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/.agents/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- remotion-best-practices: Best practices for Remotion - Video creation in React (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/remotion-best-practices/SKILL.md)
- speech: Use when the user asks for text-to-speech narration or voiceover, accessibility reads, audio prompts, or batch speech generation via the OpenAI Audio API; run the bundled CLI (`scripts/text_to_speech.py`) with built-in voices and require `OPENAI_API_KEY` for live calls. Custom voice creation is out of scope. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/speech/SKILL.md)
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/nileshteji/dot-files/codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)

  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.

package com.leapscholar.app.experience.core

interface UserExperience :
    LoginFlowConfig,
    DashboardNavConfig,
    ShortlistViewConfig,
    OnboardingFormConfig,
    ChatUiConfig
# Experience Core Context

## Purpose
Defines stable contracts that feature modules consume to get segment-aware behavior without knowing segment rules.

## Files and Meaning
- `UserExperience`: aggregate type used by DI module to expose all contracts.
- `LoginFlowConfig`: maps backend/user state to `LeapLoginState`.
- `DashboardNavConfig`: controls dashboard bottom-nav tabs/start destination.
- `ShortlistViewConfig`: controls shortlist UI toggles and labels.
- `OnboardingFormConfig`: controls onboarding form ids used by tofu flow.
- `ChatUiConfig`: controls chat title/theme behavior.

## Rules
- Keep this package interface-only.
- No direct dependency on `UserDataUtils`, repositories, activities, or Compose.
- Any new contract should be cohesive and narrow.

## Consumers
- `DashboardViewModel` (`DashboardNavConfig`)
- `ShortlistViewModel` (`ShortlistViewConfig`)
- `TofuFormViewModel` / `TofuFormRepository` (`OnboardingFormConfig`)
- `LeapAiViewModel` (`ChatUiConfig`)
- `UserLoginStateBuilder` via `LoginFlowResolver` (`LoginFlowConfig`)
